summary: The article provides a comprehensive guide for building infrastructure platforms that enable product teams to focus on building solutions rather than managing hosting infrastructure. It argues that organizations should start with a clear problem statement and measurable goal before investing in platform teams. The authors emphasize treating the platform as a product with real users (developers), conducting proper discovery, onboarding users early for feedback, and avoiding over-complication. Success depends on creating a great user experience that teams want to adopt voluntarily rather than being mandated to use.
key_points: Define a clear problem statement and measurable goal before building a platform - without this, executives may scrap the initiative when it fails to demonstrate value
Treat developers as customers by conducting proper discovery research, including Event Storming sessions to understand their pain points and needs
Onboard users as early as possible using 'Shortest Path to Value' thinking rather than waiting for a fully viable product
Communicate technical vision early using C4 Diagrams and Architectural Decision Records to prevent duplicate efforts and build stakeholder confidence
Keep the platform simple - every component adds maintenance burden and potential failure modes, and Conway's Law can introduce unnecessary complexity
money_quotes: By building infrastructure platforms you can save time for product teams, reduce your cloud spend and increase the security and rigour of your infrastructure.
If you can't put together a problem statement maybe you don't need an infrastructure platform.
Many organisations build platform infrastructure, and then can't get any teams to use them.
When you're developing an infrastructure platform that other teams are dependent upon; your customers' dev environments are your production environments.
Things don't always go so well if you have to mandate the usage of your infra products, so you're going to have to actually make an effort to make people want to use your product.
All software is broken. Not to put too much of a downer on things, but every line of code that you write has a very high chance of becoming quickly obsolete.
Every moving part you develop is an investment in post-live support and another potential failure mode.
key_insight: Infrastructure platforms should be treated as products with developers as customers, requiring clear goals, user research, early feedback, and simplicity to succeed rather than being mandated top-down.
